title: Angular Signals
summary: Signals, computed, effect, signal inputs/outputs, and fine-grained change detection.
guide:
# Angular Signals

Signals (stable Angular 17) are reactive value containers. Angular tracks which template expressions read a signal and updates only those — surgical vs Zone.js broad-checking everything. Think of signals as **spreadsheet cells**: a cell has a value, other cells can reference it, and downstream cells auto-update when it changes.

```
Zone.js approach:                 Signals approach:
  Any async event                   count.set(5)
       ↓                                ↓
  Check EVERY component            Mark only watchers of count
  top to bottom                    Update those bindings only
  O(n) component tree              O(k) listeners
```

## Creating and Reading Signals
```typescript
const count = signal(0);       // WritableSignal<number>
count();                        // read: 0  — CALL the function!
count.set(5);                   // replace
count.update(v => v + 1);      // compute from old: 6
```

## computed — Lazy Derived Values
```typescript
const doubled = computed(() => count() * 2);  // memoized
// Recalculates ONLY when count() changes
// Lazy: only evaluates when someone reads it
```

```
count (signal)  →  doubled (computed)  →  template
   signal(3)            () => count()*2       {{ doubled() }}
        ↓                    lazy
   count.set(4)         invalidated (not yet recalculated)
                              ↓
                        template reads doubled()
                        → recalculated: 8
```

## effect — Side Effects
```typescript
constructor() {
  effect(() => {
    document.body.className = this.theme();
    // Re-runs whenever theme() changes
  });
  // Must be called in injection context
}
```
**Use effect() for**: storage, analytics, non-Angular DOM.
**NOT for**: deriving values (use computed instead).

## Signal Inputs / Outputs (Angular 17.1+)
```typescript
label    = input<string>();             // InputSignal<string | undefined>
disabled = input(false);               // with default
size     = input.required<'sm'|'lg'>(); // required
clicked  = output<void>();             // OutputEmitterRef
value    = model(0);                   // two-way: [(value)]
```

## Bridging RxJS
```typescript
users    = toSignal(this.svc.users$(), { initialValue: [] });  // Observable → Signal
results$ = toObservable(searchTerm).pipe(debounceTime(300), switchMap(s => this.search(s)));
```

## Signals + OnPush: The Optimal Combo
```
Component(OnPush)
  count = signal(0)
  doubled = computed(() => count() * 2)
  template: {{ doubled() }}

  count.set(5)  →  Angular marks component dirty (not immediate)
                →  On next CD cycle, only this component checked
                →  doubled() recalculated: 10
                →  DOM patched
```

## Common Pitfalls
- Read with `()`: `count()` not `count`.
- Arrays: `arr.update(a => [...a, item])` not `arr().push(item)`.
- `effect()` must be in injection context.
- Never `effect(() => other.set(...))` — use `computed()`.
- `toSignal` initial value: undefined by default if no `initialValue` — causes null-check issues in template.
- `untracked(() => signalName())` reads a signal without creating a dependency in computed/effect.

questions.prompt: Primary advantage of signals over Zone.js?
choices: Fine-grained: Angular knows exactly which expressions to re-evaluate when each signal changes
questions.explanation: Zone.js checks everything; signals carry targeted 'I changed' notifications.

questions.prompt: What does count() return?
computed() recalculates:
choices: Only when a signal it reads changes — lazy and memoized
questions.explanation: computed() tracks reads. Recalculates on those signals changing only.

questions.prompt: Why use computed() not effect() for derived values?
choices: effect() → set() creates cycles; computed() is the correct reactive derivation tool
questions.explanation: effect(() => total.set(a() * b())) = cycle. Use computed(() => a() * b()).

questions.prompt: What does model() provide?
choices: Two-way bindable signal — parent binds with [(value)], component reads and updates
questions.explanation: model() replaces @Input + @Output valueChange pair with a single two-way signal.

questions.prompt: Where can effect() safely be called?
choices: Injection context — constructor, field initializer, or runInInjectionContext
questions.explanation: effect() registers cleanup on destroy via the injection context.

questions.prompt: What does toSignal(obs$) do?
choices: Creates a Signal reflecting latest Observable value; auto-subscribes and auto-unsubscribes
questions.explanation: toSignal bridges RxJS → Signals. Synchronously readable in templates.

questions.prompt: Valid ways to mutate a WritableSignal?
questions.explanation: set, update, mutate — the three mutation methods. .next() is RxJS.

questions.prompt: Valid use cases for effect()?
questions.explanation: effect() for side effects. computed() for derived values.

questions.prompt: Correct way to add an item to an array signal?
questions.explanation: Signals track by reference. Push mutates in place — same reference, no update triggered.

questions.prompt: What does `untracked(() => count())` do inside a computed?
choices: Throws error — untracked not allowed in computed
Reads count without creating a dependency — computed won't re-run when count changes
Makes count read-only inside the computed
questions.explanation: untracked() reads a signal without registering a dependency. Useful when you need a value but don't want re-computation on its change.

questions.prompt: What logs?
questions.explanation: computed is lazy — recalculates on first read after a dependency changes. a.set(3) invalidates b; b() triggers recalc: 3 * 10 = 30.

questions.prompt: toSignal(obs$) without initialValue. What is the signal's value before obs$ emits?
questions.explanation: Default initialValue is undefined. In strict templates this causes type errors unless you provide initialValue or mark the type as T | undefined.

questions.prompt: Signal input vs @Input() decorator — key advantage?
choices: Signal input is faster to type
Signal input returns an InputSignal — reactive, readable in template/computed without ngOnChanges
Signal input supports default values; @Input does not
questions.explanation: @Input requires ngOnChanges to react to changes. InputSignal is a readable signal: use in computed() or template directly.

questions.prompt: The best Angular 17+ combo for performance is:
choices: OnPush + signals — signals notify Angular which component tree node to update
questions.explanation: OnPush alone limits checks to inputs/events. Signals add surgical intra-component tracking — only the exact DOM binding updates.

questions.prompt: Which are TRUE about computed()?
choices: Memoized — same value returned until dependencies change
questions.explanation: computed() is read-only, lazy, memoized. It evaluates only when read after a dependency change — not eagerly. It can depend on other computed signals.

questions.prompt: model() in a component replaces which pattern?
choices: @Input() value + @Output() valueChange — the traditional two-way binding pair
questions.explanation: model() creates a WritableSignal that the parent binds with [(modelName)]. Internally emits a change event on .set().

questions.prompt: What happens at runtime?
choices: Works fine — field initializers are injection context
Runtime error: effect() called outside injection context
questions.explanation: Field initializers in Angular components run inside the injection context. Both constructor body and field initializers are valid for effect().

questions.prompt: How do you stop an effect from running?
choices: Effects auto-stop only on component destroy
questions.explanation: effect() returns an EffectRef. Call ref.destroy() to manually stop it. It also auto-cleans up with the injector on component destroy.

questions.prompt: toObservable(signal) emits:
choices: Once with initial value then never
Each time the signal's value changes — synchronously in the same microtask
In an async scheduled microtask after each signal change
questions.explanation: toObservable schedules emissions via effect() under the hood. Emissions happen asynchronously — not in the same synchronous flush as the signal set().

flashcards.back: set(val): replace. update(fn): compute from old. mutate(fn): in-place (deprecated). Always read by calling: count() not count.
Lazy, memoized, auto-tracked. Only recalculates when a read signal changes. Read-only — no set/update.
Side effects only. Injection context required. Never set another signal inside — use computed() for derivations.
input<T>(): optional. input(default). input.required<T>(). Returns InputSignal — reactive, no ngOnChanges needed.
toSignal(obs$, { initialValue: [] }): Observable → Signal. toObservable(sig): Signal → Observable (for RxJS pipelines). Both require injection context.
Optimal combo — OnPush + signals. Angular tracks template signal reads. Updates only affected components. No markForCheck() needed.
untracked(() => sig()) reads a signal without registering it as a dependency. Use in computed/effect when you need a value but don't want re-run on its change.
model(initialValue): WritableSignal that parent binds with [(name)]. Replaces @Input() value + @Output() valueChange pair. Component calls value.set() to emit upstream.

project.brief: Design a live dashboard: (1) metrics Observable → toSignal; (2) selectedId: writable signal; (3) selectedMetric: computed from both; (4) theme toggle → effect to localStorage; (5) search: toObservable(term) → debounce → switchMap → toSignal. Where are signals vs computed vs effect?

checklist.text: metrics = toSignal(svc.getMetrics$(), { initialValue: [] }) — Observable bridged to Signal
selectedId = signal<string|null>(null) — writable, user updates on click
effect(() => localStorage.setItem('theme', theme())) — legitimate side effect

hints: effect() must NOT set another signal — that's computed()'s domain.
All toSignal/toObservable calls go in constructor (injection context).
selectedMetric defaults to metrics()[0] if selectedId() is null.
